data.title: WD-40 Company 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by WD-40 Company in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Netflix Q3: Launch Of In-House Advertising Platform By FY 2025 Bodes Well
data.description:
I assigned a ‘Strong Buy’ rating to Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) in my previous article published in July 2024, highlighting the company’s progress and growth in ads-tier memberships. Netflix posted a strong result in Q3 with more than 5 million new subscribers. I favor their efforts to build an in-house advertising platform, which could potentially turn Netflix into an advertising technology company. Therefore, I reiterate a ‘Strong Buy’ rating with a fair value of $830 per share.

Launching In-house Advertising Platform by FY25

In 2022, Netflix announced to utilize Microsoft’s (MSFT) global advertising technology and sales partner in expanding their advertising business. However, in 2024, Netflix decided to develop their in-house advertising platform, with plans to launch by the end of 2025. By having its own platform, Netflix will likely enable marketers to fully access their subscriber base without relying on intermediary platforms. As such, Netflix could potentially provide better user experience for these marketers, and further monetize Netflix’s growing subscriber base.

On the downside, Netflix will need to invest heavily in developing their own digital advertising IT infrastructure, which may add more operating expenses for their advertising business in the near term.

On August 20th, Netflix closed deals with all major holding companies as well as independent agencies with 150%+ increase in upfront ad sales commitments over 2023. In addition, Netflix has partnered with major programmatic advertising platforms including The Trade Desk (TTD), Google Display & Video 360 and Xandr. With these ongoing investments and efforts, I anticipate Netflix is on its way to becoming a major player in the advertising technology industry.

Q3 Result and Outlook

Netflix released their Q3 result on October 17th after the market closes, reporting 15% revenue growth and 14.4% paid membership growth with beating market expectations, as illustrated in the chart below.

My biggest impression for the quarter is their 35% quarter-over-quarter growth in ads membership, which highlights the potential of the advertising business to attract more subscribers and accelerate revenue growth. The management emphasized that their in-house ads tech platform is on track to launch in Canada in Q4 and more broadly in 2025.

Netflix’s growth will be driven by the overall increase in streaming penetration as well as their market share gains. As depicted in the chart below, the streaming market continues to capture market share from cable market. I am confident that more people will cut the cable and switch to online streaming in the near future.

Netflix has experienced strong subscriber growth thanks to their crackdown on password sharing and expansions into international markets. In addition, I anticipate Netflix will grow their advertising business in the future, particularly with the launch of its own advertising technology platform in FY25. The advertising business could provide additional growth opportunities and expand the total addressable market.

For Netflix’s normalized growth, I continue to anticipate their revenue will grow by 15% annually, driven by 8% subscriber growth, 2% price growth and 5% advertising market growth. The subscriber and price growth are aligned with their recent trends. I assume their in-house advertising platform could further accelerate their advertising business, as it will enable marketers to more effectively access Netflix’s subscriber base for targeted marketing.

As discussed in my previous article, Netflix has already passed the peak of content investments. The company spent $17.7 billion on contents in FY21, $16.8 billion in FY22, then reduced to $12.5 billion in FY23. I project 80bps margin expansion, driven by 10bps from gross profits, 10bps from SG&A leverage and 60bps from decrease in content cost as a percentage of total revenue.

The WACC is calculated to be 10.2% assuming risk free rate 3.6%; beta 1.55; cost of debt 5%; equity risk premium 7%; equity $20.5 billion; debt $14.5 billion; tax rate 15%. The DCF can be summarized as follows:

Discounting all the free cash flow, the fair value is calculated to be $830 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risks

I think the biggest risk for Netflix comes from Disney (DIS). Disney has been bundling their Disney+ with ESPN and Hulu. With the multiple sources of contents, Disney is able to provide a broad range of subscription choices for their customers. With these bundles, subscribers can potentially achieve more value from the streaming services. I recommend Netflix’s investors to closely monitor Disney’s performance.

End Note

Netflix is becoming an advertising technology company while maintaining their core streaming business. The in-house technology platform will enable Netflix to further monetize their growing subscriber base, in my view. I reiterate a ‘Strong Buy’ rating with a fair value of $830 per share.

data.title: These REITs Trade At Premiums, But Should They?
data.description:
REITs are often thought of in a monolithic fashion. A piece of economic news will come out and a pundit on CNBC will say:

"That's good for REITs"

I find that to be an odd perspective because there are so many distinct property types within REITs and any given piece of news impacts each property type differently.

So I'm seeing that same piece of news and thinking:

"That's good for this handful of property types, neutral for another property type and bad for a particular subset."

I suppose on a weighted average basis that would mean it is good for REITs broadly, but that broad brushstroke view loses so much granularity.

With so much of the market viewing REITs from that thousand-foot perspective, there is often substantial mispricing between the property types. Fundamental strengths and weaknesses are not properly priced in, leaving some sectors substantially overvalued and others significantly undervalued. Below is the average (unweighted mean) AFFO multiple of each major REIT property sector.

  • Hotel - 10.6X AFFO.
  • Diversified - 13.8X AFFO.
  • Office - 14.5X AFFO.
  • Healthcare - 14.9X AFFO.
  • Retail - 16.7X AFFO.
  • Tower - 18.2X AFFO.
  • Apartments - 18.9X AFFO.
  • Industrial - 20.0X AFFO.
  • Storage - 20.4X AFFO.
  • Manufactured Housing - 21.6X AFFO.
  • Single Family Rental - 21.7X AFFO.
  • Data Center - 24.37X AFFO.
  • Timber - 27.4X AFFO.
  • Farmland - 39.23X AFFO.

7 sectors are below 20X and the other 7 at or above 20X. This article will focus on the sectors trading at a premium, and we will follow up later with an analysis of the discounted sectors.

If each sector were priced correctly, the higher multiple sectors would have faster growth and/or lower risk. Think of the multiple as the bar a given REIT's fundamentals have to hurdle. The higher the multiple, the better the fundamentals have to be to justify it.

As we discuss each sector, the goal is to discuss broad over or undervaluation as well as to point out anomalous pricing within the sectors.

Industrial REITs

At 20X AFFO, Industrial is the cheapest of the premium sectors, which would not have been the case until recently. Relative underperformance of industrial in recent quarters is warranted in my opinion because fundamentals have weakened considerably.

New supply has been high while incremental demand has been reduced by churn resulting in overall decreased occupancy. The weakness is most apparent in large footprint logistics warehouses which is the asset type most heavily developed.

Note that while industrial fundamentals are materially weakened, there is still strength here. Mark-to-market leasing spreads remain enormous in the 20%-80% range depending on the vintage of expiring leases. Essentially, what has changed is that it was formerly a growing mark-to-market with asking rents marching upward, but now that asking rent has stagnated it puts a timer on the rapid growth. Eventually, existing leases will catch up to market rates and without further rate growth, the mark-to-market opportunity will not be extended. Thus, rapid AFFO growth for the sector now has a ~5-year runway as opposed to its former 5-10+ year runway.

With more normalized growth, I think the sector is correctly priced at just a slight premium.

Within the sector, however, there are significant gaps in both fundamentals and valuation. The cheaper industrial REITs happen to be the ones less exposed to the new supply, making STAG Industrial, Inc. (STAG) and Plymouth Industrial REIT, Inc. (PLYM) opportunistic within the sector.

Storage REITs - 20.4X AFFO

Storage is a tiny sector by company count, but not so small when it comes to market capitalization.

As self-storage is trading at essentially the same multiple as industrial, I think it is useful to compare the growth of each sector.

A 20X multiple implies the market is anticipating a significant amount of growth. In industrial there is good visibility in the growth. If an existing lease is at $6 per square foot and market rates on similar properties are at $9 per square foot, it is quite reasonable to anticipate that when the lease expires it will be renewed at closer to the $9 market rate for 50% growth.

I find the assumed growth in self-storage to be much more speculative as the mark-to-market dynamic is the opposite.

Existing self-storage leases are at price points substantially above street asking rates. Initial rents are low to get customers in the door at which point they use the hassle of moving to keep customers in while they jack rates up.

That dynamic worked wonderfully for self-storage in the past 25 years while the sector was undersupplied. Customers had few convenient options. Today, however, self-storage is dramatically oversupplied such that nearly every operator is offering extreme discounting to get customers in the door. It is similar to cell phone plans where customers are incented to switch providers every couple of years to refresh the new customer discounts.

Thus, I don't think the REITs will have as much success as they had in the past at locking customers in for 8 years at ever higher rental rates. As such, I see the sector as overvalued because it is pricing in growth that has low visibility.

There is minimal valuation or fundamental variance within the sector, so I don't see a way to play the individual stocks either. For me, self-storage is either a no-touch or a short opportunity.

Manufactured housing - 21.6X AFFO

This is a sector that absolutely deserves to trade at a premium. Manufactured housing REITs have been consistently topping the list for the highest same-store NOI growth.

There are 2 underlying strengths of the sector which, I think, will make it a long-term outperformer.

  1. Large affordability gap - the cost of manufactured housing compared to site-built or apartments is so much cheaper that MH prices/rents can go up another 50% and still be competitive.
  2. Constrained supply.

Real estate is usually capped in its growth by developers coming in and stealing growth from incumbent properties. Manufactured housing is known to be particularly difficult to get zoned which has kept new supply in check and funneled growth to existing communities.

At current pricing, I think MH is a great sector in which to invest. Even with the somewhat high 21.6X multiple, the growth is strong enough to outperform.

Within the sector, there are cheaper ways to access the same growth fundamentals.

UMH Properties, Inc. (UMH) comes at a slightly discounted 19.9X multiple and its same-store NOI growth has actually been superior to Equity LifeStyle Properties, Inc. (ELS) and Sun Communities, Inc. (SUI).

Flagship Communities Real Estate Investment Trust (OTCPK:MHCUF) has been flying under the radar with its Canadian domicile and is what I would consider absurdly cheap at 12.7X 2025 estimated AFFO.

We discuss Flagship in more depth here. The main idea is that it has essentially the same growth as the rest of the sector, but illiquidity, small size and some potential tax hassles cause it to trade at a massive discount. If an investor has the skills and patience to jump through a few hoops the value proposition is impressive.

Single Family Rental -21.7X AFFO

Single family rental REITs sit in a niche between apartments and homes where they provide tenants with the homeowner lifestyle but are more accessible to those who can't afford the massive down payment.

In recent years they have benefitted from 2 factors:

  1. Apartment rents have risen substantially giving SFR the greenlight to raise rents as well.
  2. Home values have soared to all-time highs making down payments even more unaffordable thereby increasing their customer base.

The result has been excellent same-store NOI growth as seen with American Homes 4 Rent (AMH).

An additional benefit of higher home prices has been asset appreciation since the SFR REITs acquired a large chunk of their assets at prices well below current value.

If the future were to be an extrapolation of the past, the SFR sector would be a great buy at 21.7X. However, the aforementioned drivers are unlikely to continue at the same pace.

Home appreciation was pulled forward by the dramatic change in interest rates which made homeowners not want to sell or move to preserve their 2% mortgages. This reduced supply, concurrent with fairly normal demand caused home prices to shoot up to all-time highs.

I see no reason to think home prices will come back down, but since so much appreciation was pulled forward, there is likely to be a catch-up period of lower than normal appreciation until the longer-run trajectory is restored.

Going forward SFR fundamentals look fine. Growth looks to be positive, but perhaps a bit slower than we would like for a 21.7X multiple. As such, we are on the sidelines at this time but will watch for either a cheaper entry point or a reacceleration of fundamentals.

Tricon Residential (TCN) was essentially the Flagship of SFR in that it was the much cheaper way to play it. It has since been bought out by Blackstone.

Data Centers - 24.37X AFFO

Data centers have ridden the AI hype train to sort of egregious valuations. There is no question that there is astronomical demand growth in data centers, and I understand why that gets the market excited.

However, most data centers are unprotected from new supply, so there is minimal mechanism to funnel demand growth to existing data centers and prevent developers from just eating their cake.

Digital Realty Trust, Inc. (DLR), for example, has had mixed success at raising rents.

They seem to be fighting a perpetual battle with tenants in that if they try to raise rent, the tenants can just leave and build their own data center.

Thus, rental rates are tied to the time value of money in relation to the cost to build data centers. One could even argue that newly developed data centers are better as they would be configured specifically for AI or whatever the intended application is.

The exception to incumbents having no advantage would be colocation centers where direct connections create a sort of network externality. By having a vibrant ecosystem of tenants with which to connect, an existing data center can potentially have an advantage over a new build.

Equinix, Inc. (EQIX) has had significant success in colocation and cross connects which to me makes them the better play within the sector.

Overall, however, I think data centers are significantly overvalued, and it is time to get off that hype train.

Timber - 27.4X AFFO and Farmland - 39.23X AFFO

At this point, I have lost count of how many times I have heard people saying they won't invest in the timber REITs or farmland REITs because they are too expensive. I suspect this perception comes from their very high AFFO multiples.

REITs in general are so consistently valued on either AFFO or FFO that it gets universally applied, even when it is not applicable.

Land is a different asset class. It gets at least half of its return from appreciation or special purposes like higher-better-use.

It just comes down to a misunderstanding of how FFO and AFFO are calculated. Depreciation is added back and gains on sale are taken out. Well, land has no depreciation and due to appreciation, HBU and other opportunistic sales, often enjoy huge gains on sale.

Thus, AFFO and FFO are materially understating true earnings.

These are actually among the most discounted REIT sectors, it just isn't obvious because of the headline numbers as they still report in AFFO.

The timberland REITs trade at about 80% of asset value and the farmland REITs trade even lower based on recent sale prices.

To get a truer AFFO figure one should add appreciation to AFFO. Land has historically appreciated north of 5% a year, but even if we use a conservative 3% appreciation on book value it shows how cheap these REITs are. Here are some back of the napkin figures:

  • Farmland Partners Inc. (FPI) - $30.8 million annual appreciation or $0.64 per share.
  • Weyerhaeuser Company (WY) - $502 million annual appreciation or $0.70 a share.
  • Gladstone Land Corporation (LAND) - $40.5 million annual appreciation or $1.13 per share.

Obviously, this calculation methodology is extremely oversimplified and there are all sorts of granular reasons to think appreciation will be higher or lower for a given set of assets, but this gets you roughly in the ballpark of true earnings.

Adding these above figures to the stated AFFO puts these REITs at quite cheap multiples.

There could be fundamental reasons not to like investing in these stocks, but don't make the mistake of thinking they are expensive just because nominal multiples are high.

Wrapping it up

REIT sector AFFO multiples are not well correlated with fundamental growth. There are plenty of traps as well as opportunities to get high growth at a cheap price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Uncovering The Risks: Why Super Micro Computer May Be A Sell
data.description:
A lot has happened since my last recommendation of Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI), also called Supermicro, on July 20, 2024. The company failed to file its 10-K for fiscal year [FY] 2024, which was due on August 29. Management received a non-compliance notification letter from Nasdaq on September 20, 2024. Although non-compliance has no immediate effect, investors may have to monitor it over the next several months.

On top of that bad news, more bad news followed in September, when The Wall Street Journal reported that the Department of Justice [DoJ] started an initial investigation into the company. The DoJ apparently became interested in Supermicro after a former employee, Bob Luong, filed a whistleblower lawsuit alleging accounting violations. When you combine all this news with a high valuation and investors' other worries about the company, the stock has been down 40.72% since my July recommendation, compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) 4.88% rise.

However, the stock now sells at a more reasonable valuation. Seeking Alpha's quant rates the valuation a B-. The stock has rebounded since October 7, when the company announced a new liquid-cooling system. It currently ships over 100,000 GPUs (Graphics Processing Units) per quarter and has delivered over 2,000 liquid-cooled racks since June 2024. So, although Supermicro still has the overhang from not filing its 10-K and a potential DoJ investigation, it doesn't appear to have scared off customer demand for its servers.

The market still has reasons for its interest in this stock. Supermicro has produced explosive revenue growth over the last three years due to massive demand for Artificial Intelligence [AI] infrastructure, and that demand shows little sign of waning. Its most significant competitors, Dell Technologies (DELL) and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) have not benefited nearly as much from the AI revolution. Analysts expect 87% annual year-over-year revenue growth and 51% annual year-over-year earnings-per-share [EPS] growth in its June Fiscal 2025-blazing hot growth.

This article will discuss the company's failure to file its annual report, the fundamentals from its fourth-quarter FY 2024 earnings release, and what investors should monitor when it reports first-quarter FY 2025 earnings near the end of October. It will also discuss the stock's valuation, risks, and why I am lowering the recommendation to a sell.

Failure to file financial statements

When Super Micro Computer announced that it would not file its annual report on time, the stock immediately sank 19% from a closing price of $54.76 on August 27 to a closing price of $44.35 on August 28. It briefly reached an intraday low of $38.28 on September 6. The company's closing price on October 15 was $47.76, so it has yet to fully recover to the price it traded at before the news.

The company's failure to file financial statements is nothing new. The NASDAQ temporarily delisted it for not filing financial statements in 2018, so there is a potential for another delisting to occur. In 2020, the Securities and Exchange Commission [SEC] charged Supermicro and its Chief Financial Officer [CFO] at the time, Howard Hideshima, with accounting violations. The SEC website stated:

The Securities and Exchange Commission today charged Super Micro Computer, Inc., a producer of computer servers, and its former CFO, Howard Hideshima, with prematurely recognizing revenue and understating expenses over a period of at least three years. According to the SEC's orders, Super Micro executives, including Hideshima, pushed employees to maximize end-of-quarter revenue, yet failed to devise and maintain sufficient internal accounting controls to accurately record revenue. As a result, the orders find, Super Micro improperly and prematurely recognized revenue, including by recognizing revenue on goods sent to warehouses but not yet delivered to customers, shipping goods to customers prior to customer authorization, and shipping misassembled goods to customers.

As for The Wall Street Journal's reporting of a current DoJ investigation, neither representatives from Supermicro nor the DoJ have gone on the record confirming the existence of an investigation in any news reports on this topic that I can find. I also cannot find any news that former employee Bob Luong's whistleblower lawsuit has been resolved.

Next, let's look at the company's fundamentals from its fourth quarter.

Company Fundamentals

Supermicro's FY 2024 annual revenue increased by 109.8% to $14.94 billion, and its fourth-quarter FY 2024 revenue grew 143% over the previous year's comparable quarter to $5.3 billion. Its quarterly revenue grew faster than NVIDIA's (NVDA) in the latest quarter. Supermicro's rapid revenue growth initially attracted me to the stock. However, NVIDIA's revenue growth is far more valuable than Supermicro's, as NVIDIA is far more profitable and has positive free cash flow [FCF]. NVIDIA recorded around 75% gross margins in its latest quarter. In comparison, Supermicro's quarterly gross margins have dropped from above 15% in the third quarter to 11.23%.

Supermicro emphasizes non-GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) metrics in its investor presentation, which adds back stock-based compensation, which only makes up 1.3% of revenue. As a result, non-GAAP metrics differ very little from GAAP.

Chief Financial Officer [CFO] David Weigand said the following on the fourth quarter FY 2024 earnings call:

The Q4 non-GAAP gross margin was 11.3% versus 15.6% in Q3 due to product and customer mix, focus on winning strategic new designs with competitive pricing and higher initial costs in ramping production of new DLC [Direct Liquid Cooling] AI GPU clusters. For fiscal year '24, the non-GAAP gross margin was 14.2% versus 18.1% for fiscal year '23. We have a path to improve gross margins to the target range of 14% to 17% as we introduce innovative platforms based on multiple new technologies from our strategic partners and improved manufacturing efficiencies on our DLC solutions.

The above commentary suggests that DLC AI GPU clusters have lower margins because of the higher costs of ramping up production. Customers buying this lower-margin DLC product reduced Supermicro's overall gross margin. Additionally, when CFO Weigand says, "winning strategic new designs with competitive pricing," it implies that the competitive environment has become more intense, and the company needed to lower prices to gain market share. He believes that margins will return to 14% to 17% as the company becomes more efficient in manufacturing its DLC solutions and once production scales up in its lower-cost plants in Malaysia and Taiwan. Supply chain issues have also hindered the company in the short term. Management believes that once the company receives enough components, such as NVIDIA's new Blackwell chip, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) fifth-generation EPYC processors, and Intel's (INTC) Xeon® 6900 processors with P-cores, it can build its most advanced servers in which it has more pricing power and should restore gross margins.

Investors should monitor the company's ability to revive gross margins. If it cannot return margins to its targeted range over the next several quarters, the company will likely become less attractive to investors.

Operating margins dropped due to lower gross margins and increased investment in Research and Development (R&D) to maintain a technological lead over competitors.

The company diluted investors this year by raising capital through a stock offering in March and one convertible bond offering in February, which raised the sequential GAAP outstanding share count from 61.4 million to 64.2 million. Investors should remember that the company instituted a ten-for-one stock split on October 1. So, post-stock split, the company should have 642 million diluted shares outstanding. It needed to raise capital because it's currently burning cash.

Supermicro's trailing 12-month [TTM] cash flow from operations [CFO] to sales dropped to -16.59%, primarily due to the need to build up inventory before manufacturing the servers, thus tying up cash. Once the servers are produced and shipped, the company has to wait a specified period before receiving payment. Here's where being in a commodity business with little negotiating power shows up. To gain business from large cloud providers, Supermicro may have to lengthen the period when payment for its servers is due, which negatively impacts the cash flow. The difference between when Supermicro must pay its suppliers and when it gets paid by its customers creates a negative cash flow.

Supermicro's TTM FCF is -2.479 billion. Although the market may understand that FCF dropping today could result in the company potentially increasing market share and maintaining high revenue growth, the lack of FCF exerts downward pressure on Supermicro's valuation. Additionally, the cash burn has resulted in the company diluting investors through capital raises because the business is not currently self-sustaining.

At the end of FY 2024, Supermicro had $1.67 billion in cash and short-term investments and $1.772 in convertible debt and term loans. The company's debt-to-equity ratio is 0.40, meaning it finances most of its operations through equity rather than debt. At the end of the June quarter, Supermicro's TTM debt-to-EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) ratio was 1.66, a moderate amount of debt compared to its operating earnings. Generally, the market gets concerned when a company's debt-to-EBITDA rises above 4.0.

Management's guidance for FY 2025 calls for the company to generate $26 billion to $30 billion in revenue, compared to analysts' estimates of $27.95 billion. If it hits the upper end of this range, Supermicro will once again double its annual revenue this fiscal year-impressive. The company also forecasts first-quarter FY 2025 revenue of $6 billion to $7 billion, compared to analysts' consensus estimates of $6.46 billion. The market loves the top-line growth but worries over profitability and FCF.

Risk

The above discussion of the company's fundamentals presupposes that the numbers management reported are valid. On September 3, Chief Executive Officer [CEO] Charles Liang released the following letter (emphasis added):

You may have seen our recent announcement that Supermicro will be delayed in filing its Annual Report for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2024, and separately, a report published by a short seller. I wanted you to hear from me directly about what they mean for you and our products. Neither of these events affects our products or our ability and capacity to deliver the innovative IT solutions that you rely on every day. Our production capabilities are unaffected and continue operating at pace to meet customer demand. Our world class engineering and support teams are also unaffected and continue to build and deploy large scale AI Total Solutions. Our liquid-cooled solutions continue to ramp. We have shipped approximately 2,000 DLC liquid-cooled AI racks to customers, which we believe is more than 75% of the entire DLC liquid-cooled AI server market this calendar year. Ultimately, we remain well-positioned to deliver our broad product portfolio to you and our thousands of other customers around the world. Our primary focus remains to help you meet your IT challenges and execute your mission. In terms of our Annual Report, the Audit Committee of the Board of Directors decided to delay the filing and, as we shared publicly, has also formed a committee to review our internal controls and other matters. The Board committee is working diligently on this thorough review. Importantly, however, when we announced the decision to delay our Annual Report filing, we indicated that based on the work done so far, we don't anticipate any material changes in our fourth quarter or fiscal year 2024 financial results. This is good news. I continue to have strong confidence in our finance and internal teams.

Usually, when a company forms a committee to review internal controls, it's to address internal control weaknesses, regulatory investigations, or corporate governance issues. In Supermicro's case, it is all three. Although CEO Liang's attempt to reassure the market has calmed the waters, there is no guarantee that when the company files its annual report, it won't make material changes to current or past filings. The longer it takes for the company to file its annual report only increases the uncertainty surrounding the stock. Also, understand that any current review of the company's metrics may not be valid, which is a significant concern to the market.

The uncertainty of a potential investigation by the DoJ, possible accounting and governance issues, a fiercely competitive market, and low profitability have combined to make the stock heavily shorted, 21.25% of the float short.

As long as the market remains concerned about the accuracy of its financial reports, Supermicro has significant downside potential.

Valuation

Supermicro's price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 1.927, well above its three- and five-year median. Some might consider the company overvalued because it trades above its P/S median.

The company's one-year forward P/S ratio is 0.879, around the top end of its range in 2023. Considering Supermicro's potential revenue growth rate due to the AI opportunity over the next year, some may consider the stock, at worst, fairly valued, and some may consider it undervalued.

Supermicro's one-year forward price-to-earnings ratio is 11.21. Down substantially from its March 2024 high of around 34. Considering that this company is in a historically low-margin business with significant competition, in hindsight, the news of the stock entering the S&P 500 index on March 18, 2024, which boosted the valuation to its 2024 highs, was probably the time for investors to take profits, instead of following my April 5 buy recommendation.

Supermicro's one-year forward Price/Earnings-to-Growth [PEG] ratio is 0.40 (One-year forward P/E of 11.21 divided by analysts' FY 2026 estimated EPS growth of 27.76%). Generally, the market considers a PEG ratio below 1.0 a sign of an undervalued stock. If it sold at a PEG ratio of one, the stock price would be $118.25, a rise of 147% above its October 11 closing price.

Investors should not expect the stock to zoom up to $118 immediately. As long as the uncertainty from a possible restatement of earnings and a DoJ investigation hangs over the stock, investors may not push the stock's valuation much higher than it is today. Wall Street analysts only have a one-year price target of $74.91, which equates to a one-year forward PEG ratio of 0.63.

Supermicro is a sell

I would recommend buying Supermicro if it had no DoJ investigation concerns or potential accounting problems. I really like the company's upside potential. However, I learned long ago that when a company comes under a potential DoJ investigation for alleged accounting irregularities, it's often better to be safe than sorry and sell the stock until the threat of a bad outcome has passed. Risk-averse investors should consider selling Supermicro despite the stock's attractive valuation. Investors who sell may miss out on potential upside if the eventual outcome favors the company. However, only investors who can withstand the potential downside due to a poor resolution to its potential accounting and legal woes should continue holding. Once the danger has passed, we can revisit the case for buying Supermicro.

data.title: Enovix: Fully Charged To Move Higher
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Enovix (NASDAQ:ENVX) is a pre-revenue business. Therefore, notwithstanding my own bullishness for this company, please remain aware that this company's prospects are still highly speculative.

Here's what Enovix is seeking to commercialize, small batteries for wearable devices for the biggest producers in the Fortune 500.

This is the background. AI is extremely power-hungry. Watching videos in 8K is extremely power-hungry. Using ChatGPT is extremely power-hungry. The penetration of AI apps is only getting started. And conventional batteries don't allow devices to survive the whole day.

Without any heroics, I now believe that Enovix's stock is the most appealing it's been for a long time. The risk-reward now is very attractive. Price target of $20 by the end of 2025. A massive upside from this price point now.

Rapid Recap

Back in July, I said:

Investing in Enovix now makes sense because the company has secured a high-profile customer in California, which signals strong demand for its high-energy density batteries.

Since then, the stock has been highly volatile but ultimately gone nowhere fast. Even though the stock has not been for the faint-hearted, I maintain that this stock is a strong buy. Particularly now. Here's why.

Latest Development

Early in October, Enovix stated that it began shipping EX-1M battery cell samples from its brand-new factory line in Malaysia. This is a massive turnaround. Recall, earlier this year, Enovix was still shipping its pilot batteries from California. The factory in Malaysia was nowhere near operational.

This means that two things. Firstly, and most importantly, that Enovix is on track for high-volume production in 2025. This is massive. The main goal post for this investment thesis is within reach and I'm just super excited.

There's still some Site Acceptance Testing for this high-volume line, but the pieces are now coming together.

What's more, I believe that the timing of this news is no accident. With their quarterly earnings in the coming weeks, management must have felt that by then it would have further good news that it gave investors a preannouncement of sorts. Specifically, it's noted that ''by shipping samples within the third quarter of 2024'', supporting my contention that Enovix is aggressively working to get up to scale as quickly as possible.

How Much More Shareholder Dilution?

As it stands right now, Enovix has roughly $200 million worth of stock. Given that Enovix is burning about $200 to $300 million per year, this means that Enovix is going to have to dilute investors one more time.

Therefore, I believe, Enovix will have to dilute investors by approximately 15% in the coming, thereby providing Enovix with enough near-term cash to get its high-volume batteries being produced and shipped out.

Meanwhile, I ask that you consider how much stock insiders were purchasing of Enovix last year at this exact price?

Several of its TJ Rodgers, Enovix's Founder stock purchases were more than $1 million, despite TJ Rodgers obviously already owning more than 10% of this company's stock, see below.

Altogether, if you are willing to give this stock a little time, like a further 12 months, I believe that Enovix is going to move to at least $17 per share in the coming year.

Now, let's be clear. The recent sale from insiders has also spooked investors, particularly as Enovix's Chief Legal Office Arthi Chakravarthy and others have set about selling more than $500K worth of stock in the past several days.

Bears have been quick to latch onto the idea that insiders are eager to cash in now, with the stock trading at an ''overvalued'' priced.

Meanwhile, for my part, I fully disregard these prearranged share sales as having any view as to the medium-term fundamentals of this business.

ENVX Valuation - 27x Non-GAAP Operating Profits

Enovix believes that in 2026, it can be delivering 50% gross margins, and approximately 30% operating margins.

Using analysts' very rough estimates for 2026, Enovix could see around $250 million in revenues, which means its non-GAAP operating profits around be $75 million. Leaving the stock priced at 20x two years out of non-GAAP operating profits.

But the issue here is that the business is scaling from approximately $0 million in revenues. And this $250 million is an estimate.

What happens if, in 2026, Enovix only reaches $100 million of revenues? Then, the stock is priced at more than 65x two years forward non-GAAP operating profits.

Accordingly, it's really difficult to get a clear sense of just how undervalued this stock is.

Investment Risks

I've already noted the potential shareholder dilution that, I believe, is necessary for Enovix to reach its near-term financial targets.

On top of that, the technology itself hasn't been fully developed and tested. Enovix still needs to get its battery yields close to 90% and beyond, and for now, they are probably around 65%.

On top of that, what Enovix's batteries deliver has to be superior to what Samsung and other mass-producers of consumer devices are able to get their hands on right now.

The Bottom Line

In conclusion, I believe that Enovix holds exciting potential despite its current pre-revenue status and the risks involved. The company's aggressive push toward high-volume production, paired with the increasing demand for power-efficient solutions in an AI-driven world, makes it a compelling opportunity.

While shareholder dilution and operational challenges remain, the bullish target of $20 per share seems achievable with some patience. I remain optimistic about the future-let's just say Enovix might be fully charged for a bright 2025.

Price target of $20 by the end of 2025.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Spikes
data.description:
Bearish sentiment among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, both optimism and neutral sentiment decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 3.6 percentage points to 45.5%. Bullish sentiment is above its historical average of 37.5% for the 49th time in 50 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 1.3 percentage points to 29.2%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 15th consecutive week.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 4.8 percentage points to 25.4%. Bearish sentiment is below its historical average of 31.0% for the ninth time in 10 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 8.4 percentage points to 20.1%. The bull-bear spread is above its historical average of 6.5% for the 23rd time in 24 weeks.

This week's special question asked AAII members how they would describe the current valuation of stocks.

Here is how they responded:

  • Stocks, in general, are overvalued: 48.7%.
  • Stocks, in general, are fairly valued: 12.1%.
  • Valuations are mixed, with some stocks expensive and others cheap: 34.5%.
  • Stocks, in general, are undervalued: 2.6%.
  • Not sure/no opinion: 1.7%.

This week's Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 45.5%, down 3.6 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 29.2%, down 1.3 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 25.4%, up 4.8 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: VIG: Turning More Expensive, But Still Attractive
data.description:
There was a modest uptick in the relative number of dividend-increase announcements in the third quarter compared to a year ago. While it’s still early in Q4, a solid dividend-hike trend appears ongoing. It comes as profits among global companies look strong heading into the new year.

Macro risks are real, though, including an uncertain US election outcome and fears of a slowdown in GDP growth following this year’s above-consensus expansion pace. Still-elevated inflation and a weakening domestic labor market are two risks. But multinational corporations boast balance sheets that are in a prime position to weather what the macro landscape might deliver in the quarters ahead.

While I grow increasingly concerned about the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF Shares’ (NYSEARCA:VIG) valuation, I reiterate a buy rating. I continue to like the fund’s diversified and high-quality portfolio. With bullish seasonality on tap, there’s likely more upside to come, but a 20x P/E should cap investors’ enthusiasm.

Rising Share of Dividend-Increase Announcements

According to the issuer, VIG seeks to track the performance of the S&P U.S. Dividend Growers Index and employs a passively managed, full replication indexing approach. The ETF contains large-cap equities, with an emphasis on owning shares of firms that grow their dividends year over year. It can be seen as a "dividend aristocrat" product.

Since my Q2 analysis and buy rating, VIG has returned 12%, about two percentage points above the S&P 500’s total return. A broad shift occurred this past July when mega-cap tech gave way to value and cyclical areas in terms of market leadership.

As it stands, VIG’s total assets under management is now $102 billion, up materially from just $90 billion in May. While the ETF’s valuation is more concerning, its annual expense ratio remains modest at just 0.06%, making it an ideal fund for long-term investors.

The current trailing 12-month dividend yield is 1.7%, about 0.4 percentage points above that of the S&P 500, and share-price momentum has been very strong over the past several months. With a stellar Risk grade from Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, VIG has a track record of producing healthy returns with modest drawdowns in the past two years.

The fund is also highly liquid – average daily volume is north of 600,000 shares while VIG’s 30-day median bid/ask spread is tight at just two basis points, per Vanguard.

For a refresher on the portfolio, the 5-star, Gold-rated ETF by Morningstar has very little growth exposure has low access to domestic SMID caps. Instead, 80% of the allocation is considered large-cap blend or large-cap value. That means VIG can offer solid diversification to the growth-heavy SPX.

But now, with a price-to-earnings ratio nearing 21, the valuation case is sketchy, particularly considering its modest 9.0% long-term EPS growth rate. Thus, I am less confident in my buy call, but I will outline other reasons why sticking with VIG appears sound for now.

VIG: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

While VIG’s stand-alone earnings multiple is no bargain, on a relative basis it’s not overly pricey. What’s more, the ETF offers solid sector diversification with holdings in Information Technology (growth), Financials (cyclical value), and Health Care (a mix of growth and value).

The 341-holding portfolio also features modest single-stock risk.

VIG: Holdings & Dividend Information

One of the bullish considerations right now is seasonality. While we didn’t see much September fear, late October through year-end is generally a bullish stretch when analyzing 10-year historical trends.

VIG: Strong October-November Trends

The Technical Take

With solid diversification and decent seasonality, momentum and technicals are strong. Notice in the chart below that VIG’s bull market is now two years old. With a rising long-term 200-day moving average, and both the shorter-term 50dma and price above the 200dma, the bulls clearly control the primary trend. What’s more, VIG’s RSI momentum indicator at the top of the graph shows a bullish RSI range between 40 and 80. There’s also an uptrend support line that comes into play near $190.

Back in May, I outlined a long idea that targeted $199. That has now been achieved with the rally in just the past few sessions. So, taking some profits may be prudent, but the technical trends still appear robust even with VIG touching an uptrend resistance line. Support is possible both at the $195 mark and near the July high between $190 and $191.

VIG: Bullish Uptrend, Upside Price Target Achieved, Strong Momentum

The Bottom Line

Despite some valuation concerns, I continue to like VIG. I reiterate a buy rating on the diversified dividend aristocrat ETF amid strong momentum and bullish seasonal trends.

data.title: Building A $25,000 Dividend Portfolio: Enhancing SCHD With October's Top 10 High-Yield Picks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This article demonstrates how to strategically build a dividend portfolio with $25,000 by enhancing the portfolio’s core position, Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD) with October’s top 10 high dividend yield companies in addition to five individually selected dividend growth companies.

The portfolio is designed to offer a superior capacity for the generation of dividend income and a broader sector and geographical diversification when compared to investing exclusively in the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF.

In previous articles on Seeking Alpha, I have already mentioned that I consider the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF to be an excellent core element of an investment portfolio, given its capacity to effectively blend dividend income and dividend growth and its ability to provide investors with a sustainable dividend.

My opinion is reflected in having incorporated SCHD as a core element within The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio, a portfolio that blends dividend income and dividend growth, and which I am monitoring and documenting on Seeking Alpha.

However, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF also has some weak points, including an underrepresentation of companies from the real estate, materials, and utilities sectors.

The Strategically Enhanced SCHD Portfolio with October's Top 10 High Dividend Yield Companies holds Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF as the core element (representing 55% of the overall portfolio). It is designed to produce a superior dividend income (Weighted Average Dividend Yield FWD] of 4.01% compared to SCHD’s Dividend Yield [FWD] of 3.79%). It also holds a broader sector and geographical diversification, effectively addressing the weaknesses of the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF.

Overview of the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF

Today, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF offers investors a Dividend Yield [TTM] of 3.41% along with a 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 12.00%, indicating to balance dividend income and dividend growth effectively.

Holdings Breakdown

Currently, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF consists of 103 different holdings.

The largest sector of SCHD is presently the financials sector, which represents 18.00%, followed by the health care sector (15.92%), the consumer defensive sector (13.74%), the energy sector (12.60%), the industrials sector (11.74%), the consumer cyclical sector (11.03%), and the information technology sector (10.38%).

The communication sector (4.82%), the materials sector (1.74%), and the utilities sector (0.04%) represent a small proportion within this ETF. The real estate sector is currently not represented within SCHD.

Through the strategic allocation of the 10 high dividend yield companies and five dividend growth companies in addition to SCHD, I aim to increase the proportions of those sectors that are underrepresented within SCHD, thereby ensuring a broader sector diversification compared to solely investing in SCHD.

In a previous article, I highlighted my top 10 high dividend yield companies to consider investing in for October 2024. I have selected the following 10 companies due to their attractive Valuation, their ability to combine dividend income and dividend growth, and their financial health:

  • Ares Capital (ARCC)
  • HSBC (HSBC)
  • Chevron (CVX)
  • Realty Income (O)
  • Verizon (VZ)
  • Altria (MO)
  • BNP Paribas (OTCQX:BNPQF)
  • Sixt (OTCPK:SIXGF)
  • The Kraft-Heinz Company (KHC)
  • Repsol (OTCQX:REPYY) (OTCQX:REPYF)

In that article, I provided a detailed explanation of why I selected those companies. Therefore, I will not repeat those details here.

In addition to the above high dividend yield companies, I have added the following five dividend growth companies to the portfolio, ensuring a balanced approach of dividend income and dividend growth for our overall portfolio:

  • NIKE (NKE)
  • Linde (LIN)
  • Apple (AAPL)
  • NextEra Energy (NEE)
  • Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG)

Nike is an excellent pick for this portfolio to enhance its dividend growth potential, given the company’s 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 10.96% and its relatively low Payout Ratio of 39.78% in addition to the company’s presently attractive Valuation.

Linde is an attractive addition to this portfolio since it helps us increase the proportion of the materials sector, which is underrepresented within the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF. Additionally, the company’s 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 13.94% indicates strong dividend growth potential, increasing the portfolio’s capacity to raise its dividend in the future.

NextEra Energy effectively combines dividend income and dividend growth, underlined by the company’s Dividend Yield [FWD] of 2.56% and its 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 10.62%. Additionally, the selection of this company allows us to increase the proportion of the utilities sector, further elevating the diversification of our dividend portfolio.

Apple helps us increase the proportion of the technology sector. The company also still offers a lot of potential for dividend growth, reflected by its low Payout Ratio of 14.76%.

Even though Alphabet has just started to pay a dividend, I expect the company to be able to provide significant dividend growth in the years ahead, given the company’s attractive growth metrics (EPS Diluted Growth [FWD] of 24.06%) in addition to its financial health and competitive advantages.

Overview of the Composition of the Strategically Enhanced SCHD Portfolio with October's Top 10 High Dividend Yield Companies

Source: The Author, data from Seeking Alpha

I have considered the following factors when determining the allocation for each company:

  • The risk-reward profile of the company
  • The company’s current Valuation
  • The sustainability of the company’s dividend
  • The company’s ability to blend dividend income with dividend growth
  • The financial health of the company
  • The company’s competitive advantages
  • The company’s growth outlook
  • The company’s competitive position within its industry

It is important to highlight that each company is within the allocation limits I set for each company in my previous article on October’s top 10 high dividend yield companies.

Additionally, through a careful selection process, my objective was to meet the following portfolio requirements:

  • No company accounts for more than 5% of the overall portfolio, even when allocating SCHD across the companies it is invested in.
  • Only companies that are particularly attractive in terms of risk and reward can account for more than 3% of the overall portfolio, ensuring a reduced company-specific concentration risk.
  • Companies with a higher risk level account for less than 3% of the overall portfolio.
  • The proportion of the real estate, materials and utilities sectors should be increased compared to only investing in SCHD, ensuring a broader sector diversification.
  • Inclusion of companies from outside the United States to increase geographical diversification while maintaining the portfolio’s main focus on U.S. companies.

Risk Analysis of the Strategically Enhanced SCHD Portfolio with October's Top 10 High Dividend Yield Companies

Risk Analysis of the Portfolio Allocation per Company/ETF

I have allocated 55% to the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF. This allocation approach not only helps us to increase the diversification and reduces the company-specific concentration risk of this portfolio but also effectively implements our investment approach to integrate dividend income and dividend growth.

With a Dividend Yield [TTM] of 3.41% and a 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 12.00%, SCHD reaches a strong balance between dividend income and dividend growth, which makes it an excellent strategic element for this portfolio.

With 5% of the overall portfolio, Realty Income represents the largest individual position of this portfolio since the company offers an attractive risk-reward profile and combines dividend income with dividend growth while also allowing us to significantly increase the proportion of the real estate sector on the portfolio, directly addressing a weak point of the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF.

Risk Analysis of the Portfolio’s Company-Specific Concentration Risk When Distributing SCHD Across its Companies

Through a careful selection process and strategic allocation approach, I have ensured that no single company accounts for a disproportionally high percentage of the overall portfolio, even when distributing SCHD across the companies it is invested in.

When allocating SCHD across its companies, Realty Income is the largest position in this dividend portfolio, accounting for 5.00% of the overall portfolio.

Chevron is the second-largest position, representing 4.26% of the portfolio. The portfolio is directly invested in Chevron (with a proportion of 2%) and indirectly via SCHD (with a proportion of 2.26%).

Nike, Linde, Ares Capital, Apple, and Alphabet each make up 4% of the overall portfolio.

I have only allocated 1% of the portfolio to Verizon to ensure that the company does not represent a disproportionally high share of the overall portfolio, considering the fact that SCHD is already invested in Verizon.

When allocating SCHD across its respective companies, Verizon represents 3.34% of our dividend portfolio, as you can see in the chart below.

NextEra Energy and HSBC each represent 3% of our overall portfolio.

All other companies make up 2.5% or less in relation to the overall portfolio. It is important to note that the chart below only includes companies that represent at least 2% of this dividend portfolio.

Risk Analysis of the Portfolio’s Sector-Specific Concentration Risk When Distributing SCHD Across its Sectors

By allocating a larger share of our dividend portfolio to Linde, I have increased the proportion of the materials sector from 1.73% (when only investing in SCHD) to 5.00% (when following the investment approach of this dividend portfolio), ensuring a broader sector diversification for our portfolio.

By adding NextEra Energy to this portfolio, I have increased the proportion of the utilities sector from 0.04% (when only investing in SCHD) to 3.03% (when following the investment approach of this dividend portfolio).

Through the incorporation of Realty Income, I have increased the proportion of the real estate sector (from previously 0% when investing only in SCHD) to 5%, allowing us to reach a broader sector diversification and giving us a portfolio that can perform well in different market scenarios.

Risk Analysis of the 15 Individual Positions of the Strategically Enhanced SCHD Portfolio with October's Top 10 High Dividend Yield Companies

The Dividend Yield [FWD]

The potential for the generation of dividend income in this portfolio is underlined by the chart below, which demonstrates the Dividend Yield [FWD] of the 15 individually added companies to complement the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity core position.

It can be highlighted that Ares Capital (Dividend Yield [FWD] of 9.33%), Altria (8.17%), BNP Paribas (7.46%), Repsol (7.15%), HSBC (6.68%) and Verizon (6.34%) significantly contribute to the generation of dividend income for this portfolio, underscoring that it is particularly suitable for dividend-income oriented investors.

The 5-Year Dividend Growth Rates [CAGR]

To ensure a balance between dividend income and dividend growth within this dividend portfolio, I have also included companies that have strong dividend growth potential, allowing us to raise the dividend to a significant amount year after year, which makes the portfolio particularly attractive for long-term investors.

The companies with the highest dividend growth rates of this dividend portfolio are Linde (5-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 13.94%), Sixt (11.54%), Nike (10.96%), and NextEra Energy (10.62%). The companies’ double-digit dividend growth rates reflect their strong dividend growth potential in the years to come.

Only The Kraft-Heinz Company and Repsol have shown a negative 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of -2.60% and -1.55%, respectively. This is one of the reasons that I have underweighted both companies in this portfolio, providing both with only 2.5% of the overall portfolio, thereby reducing the risk level of the portfolio.

The Equity Style

The equity style of this dividend portfolio further underlines its low-risk level, providing investors with elevated chances for successful investment outcomes.

The chart below demonstrates that 47% of the selected companies are large-cap companies with a value focus, and 21% are large-cap companies with a core focus (combining value and growth).

17% are mid-cap companies with a value focus, and 12% are mid-cap companies with a core focus.

Only 2% are small-cap companies with a value focus, and 1% are small-cap companies with a core focus.

There are no pure growth companies included in this portfolio, which underscores the reduced risk level of this dividend portfolio and the elevated likelihood for positive investment outcomes.

Geographical Diversification

The chart below further demonstrates the geographical diversification of this dividend portfolio. While SCHD is only invested within the U.S., I have further geographically diversified this dividend portfolio through the incorporation of companies outside the U.S.

While the primary focus remains on the U.S. (86.5%), 7% is allocated to companies in the United Kingdom (3% to HSBC and 4% to Linde), 2.5% to Spain (Repsol), 2% to France (BNP Paribas), and 2% to Germany (Sixt).

This investment approach allows us to reach a broader geographical diversification when compared to only investing in SCHD, and further reduces our portfolio’s risk level while maintaining a focus on the U.S.

Conclusion

This article has demonstrated how to build a $25,000 dividend portfolio by enhancing SCHD with October’s top 10 high dividend yield companies and five individually selected dividend growth companies.

This portfolio offers investors a superior capacity for the generation of dividend income (indicated by the portfolio’s Weighted Average Dividend Yield [FWD] of 4.01% compared to SCHD’s Dividend Yield [FWD] of 3.79%).

With a 5-Year Weighted Average Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 9.06%, the portfolio also demonstrates strong dividend growth potential.

The portfolio further provides investors with a broader sector diversification by incorporating companies from the materials sector (Linde), the utilities sector (NextEra Energy) and the real estate sector (Realty Income), which are underrepresented within SCHD.

Additionally, the portfolio has been geographically diversified by adding companies from outside the U.S., such as the United Kingdom (HSBC and Linde), Spain (Repsol), France (BNP Paribas) and Germany (Sixt), while maintaining its primary focus on the U.S. market where we can find the companies with the most attractive risk-reward profiles.

The broader sector and geographical diversification help us to decrease the portfolio’s risk level compared to only investing in SCHD, allowing a strategic portfolio positioning for different market scenarios.

This dividend portfolio, which uses SCHD as a core position (representing 55% of the overall portfolio), is designed to deliver attractive risk-adjusted long-term results, balancing stable dividend income with strong potential for dividend growth and capital appreciation. It aims to generate a constantly increasing amount of dividends year over year, allowing us to increase our wealth steadily.

What do you think about this investment approach that mixes SCHD with individual companies? Are you following a similar investment strategy? How do you approach enhancing core ETFs like SCHD with individual picks?

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TSMC Q3: Strong Growth In Server AI Processor Market
data.description:
I assigned a "Buy" rating to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited aka TSMC (NYSE:TSM) in September 2024, emphasizing their 2nm and A16 roadmap. During the Q3 FY24 earnings call, the management indicated very strong demand for server AI processors, which they anticipate will account for 15% of total revenue in FY24. I reiterate a "Buy" rating with a one-year target price of US$240 per share.

TSMC's 2nm Fabrication Process for iPhone 18

As reported by the media, Apple Inc. (AAPL) is poised to make a significant change with the iPhone 18 in 2026, leveraging TSMC's 2nm fabrication process for their A20 chip. TSMC's management indicated that the mass production of their 2nm process will begin in 2025. In addition, it was reported that TSMC demonstrated its initial 2nm chips to Apple back in December 2023. Given this timeline, I think it is quite possible for Apple to adopt TSMC's cutting-edge 2nm fabrication process for the 2026 iPhone.

Apple's A20 chipset is also expected to change its packaging process from an Integrated Fan-Out to a Wafer-Level Multi-Chip Module, enabling the integration of different chips in the same package process. The design change could significantly enhance the iPhone's communication speed, delivering substantial improvement for end-users. I think it is a very positive catalyst for TSMC, showcasing their technology leadership in their 2nm process. As discussed in my previous article, the 2nm and A16 are critical components of TSMC's product roadmap, enabling the company to maintain their leadership position in the future.

Q3 and Outlook

TSMC released their Q3 result on October 17th, reporting 39% revenue growth, driven by strong performance in the high-performance computing segment, as shown in the chart below.

The strong performance was primarily driven by their 3nm and 5nm technologies, which together accounted for more than 52% of total wafer revenue during the quarter. My biggest takeaway from the quarter is the management's confidence in AI growth. As indicated over the earnings call, TSMC's management anticipates revenue from server AI processors will triple this year, reaching mid-teens as a percentage of total revenue for FY24. The strong growth in the AI processor business reflects robust demand across GPUs, AI training and inference workloads as well as other related segments such as AI servers, HBMs and custom silicon chips. Due to the strong performance, TSMC is guiding for around 33.5% revenue growth in FY24.

Valuation

Considering the strong growth YTD, I don't anticipate any earnings surprises for FY24. As such, I raised my FY24 revenue growth rate forecast to 33.5%, aligned with their guidance. For growth from FY25 onwards, I am considering the following factors:

  • I continue to anticipate the overall foundry market will grow by mid-single-digit through the cycle, primarily driven by AI chips, automotive semiconductors as well as industrial automations.
  • TSMC's 2nm and A16 will likely begin driving their revenue business from FY25. I project these new advanced processes will contribute an additional 2% growth to the overall topline. During the earnings call, the management noted that A16 is very attractive for AI server chips, with strong demand expected for both the 2nm and A16 processes.
  • TSMC has been gaining market share from Intel Corporation (INTC) and other small players. I forecast a 1% growth contribution from their market share gains.

As such, I model 8% organic revenue growth from FY25 onwards. I also revised my margin expansion to 30bps, driven by 15bps from gross profit improvement due to revenue mix towards a more advanced process, 10bps from SG&A operating leverage and 5bps from utilization improvement. As indicated in the earning call, TSMC has benefited from capacity utilization improvement in FY24. In addition, their 2nm and A16 are expected to carry a much higher gross margin once they enter the mass production phase.

The cost of equity is calculated to be 11% assuming: a risk-free rate of 3.6%; a beta of 1.16; and an equity risk premium of 6.5%. The free cash flow from equity (FCFE) is calculated as follows. Please note, the currency in the table below is New Taiwan Dollars in thousands.

Discounting all the future FCFE, the one-year target price is calculated to be US$240 per share, as per my estimates. The FX used in the model is 1 USD = 32 Taiwan Dollar.

Key Risk

On October 14th, Bloomberg reported that the US government has discussed capping sales of advanced AI chips from NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) to some countries such as China. As both Nvidia and AMD are TSMC's key customers, any potential export restrictions would pose some near-term challenges for TSMC's growth.

As discussed in my initiation report, the US government is pushing Nvidia and Apple to utilize Intel Corporation's (INTC) foundry capabilities. Currently, it is difficult to quantify the impact of these geopolitical risks on TSMC.

End Note

I am confident that TSMC's 2nm and A16 will become a significant growth driver for the company in the near future. TSMC is leading the competition in the global foundry market. I reiterate a "Buy" rating with a one-year target price of US$240 per share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: M&T Bank: Powering Higher On Strong Results
data.description:
We continue our earnings coverage of regional banks today with M&T Bank Corporation (NYSE:MTB), a larger regional firm that just reported Q3 earnings. The stock has really had a run this year, and has gone from a high-yield income name, to one that offers just 2.6% because of the sizable capital gains. We believe rate cuts will lead to the cost of funds falling while loan yields hold up over the longer term, so long as the rate cuts are slow and orderly. It is rapid large changes that really impact margins. Further, as rates abate, one would imagine loan demand picks back up, which should benefit the non-interest income sources, such as fees for mortgages and other loan originations.

We have maintained that MTB was a good long-term hold, and with valuation getting stretched here, we need to see some marked improvement in asset quality and efficiency metrics, while seeing loans and deposits grow to see more share appreciation. Let us discuss.

Earnings strong in Q3

In the quarter, the bank saw deposits dip and minimal growth in loans. That is a negative, but the earnings power was strong. Revenues were flat from last year, with M&T Bank bringing in $2.34 billion. The provision for credit losses was up to $120 million this quarter, down from $150 million last year. Non-interest income perked up to $606 million from $560 million last year. Net interest income was about flat from Q2, but return metrics improved.

M&T Bank saw net income of $721 million, or $4.02 per share. On an adjusted basis, EPS was $4.08. This was well above consensus expectations by $0.46, driven largely by lower provisions for credit losses and modest expenses.

Total deposits were $161.5 billion at the recent quarter-end, down from $163.5 billion in the sequential second quarter. Loan growth has stalled a bit. Total loans were $134.7 billion compared with $135.6 billion in Q2, so very minimal growth, but loans are up from $132.6 billion a year ago. Net interest margin improved, a key indicator, coming in at 3.62%. This was up from 3.59% in Q2, though still lower than last year's 3.79%. We expect margins to be more stable than in the past now that rates have held firm and only just started to be cut. As long as the cuts are orderly, you should not see extreme volatility in margins.

Asset quality improves

Loans are up, which is great, but we are laser-focused on asset quality in the sector. We saw provisions were down, which is a positive. We also saw loan charge-offs relax to $120 million, falling from $137 million in the linked quarter. However, this is still up from $96 million last year. Thus, net charge-offs were 0.35% in the quarter, up from 0.29% last year. We like the sequential improvement, but the elevations are a reflection of more loans and underlying weaknesses in some borrowers. However, it is well within expectations. Non-accrual has greatly improved. They totaled $1.93 billion at the end of Q3 and this is down 18% from a year ago, while dipping 5% from the linked quarter. Non-accrual loans were 1.42% of total loans. The allowance for credit losses has held firm at $2.2 billion, or 1.62% of all loans.

Now, we want to remind you that one of the reasons we have liked M&T Bank is that it is a highly efficient bank. The efficiency ratio hit 55%, which is better than we saw a few quarters ago when it was over 60%. It also improved from 55.3% in Q2. Moreover, the return metrics are strong. The return on average assets and equity have shown strength. The return on average assets improved to 1.45% from 1.31% in Q2, and is up from 1.41% in the year ago quarter. The return on average equity ticked up to 15.47% vs. 15.27% in Q2 and is up from 11.7% to start 2024. All in all, solid results.

Final thoughts

This stock remains a quality hold. The dividend is strong, though the yield has come down as investors have seen their principal shoot much higher this year. M&T Bank Corporation's Q3 was strong, with impressive earnings, improved asset quality, and increased efficiency. We see few signs in this report of trouble for the U.S. Economy or M&T Bank's customers as a whole.
